mapping.a_element: Rademacher complexity of a regenerating Markov chain (supremum of an empirical process indexed by a distribution/function class)
mapping.b_element: Gaussian process indexed by shallow circuits (supremum of a normalized-energy random process over the circuit class)

mapping.a_element: DKW-type uniform concentration inequality for the empirical distribution of the chain
mapping.b_element: uniform control of the circuit-indexed Gaussian process bounding the energy attainable at shallow depth

mapping.a_element: penalised adaptive clustering that recovers the change points (paper A's governing mechanism)
mapping.b_element: no correspondent: circuit-depth lower-bound argument converting the process bound into an impossibility result

brief_justification: Both papers share one central analytic tool: uniform control of the supremum of a random process (Rademacher complexity of a Markov chain in A, a Gaussian process indexed by shallow circuits in B), and Rademacher/Gaussian complexities are essentially the same object. But the governing machinery diverges: A is a positive statistical-estimation pipeline whose engine is penalised adaptive clustering that recovers change points, whereas B is a complexity-theoretic impossibility argument that turns the process bound into a circuit-depth lower bound; neither core mechanism has a role in the other, so the overlap is a shared subroutine, not shared governing machinery.
